scenes.title: Welcome to Plant Land
scenes.narration: Welcome, explorer! Today we are entering the magical world of plants. Plants are everywhere around us — in gardens, forests, farms, and even on your windowsill. They give us food, oxygen, shade, and so much more. Are you ready to discover their secrets?

dialogue.speaker: Tara
dialogue.text: I'm Tara, your learning guide! Together we'll explore how plants grow from tiny seeds into mighty trees. Let's begin our adventure!

scenes.fun_fact: The tallest tree in the world is a coast redwood named Hyperion — it stands over 115 meters tall!

scenes.title: Meet Buddi the Seed
scenes.narration: Meet Buddi — a tiny seed with a big dream. Buddi wants to grow into a strong and healthy tree, but first, Buddi needs YOUR help to understand what's inside a seed and how seeds grow into plants.

dialogue.speaker: Buddi
dialogue.text: Hello friend! I'm Buddi the seed. I may look small, but there's a whole baby plant inside me! Can you help me on my journey to become a big tree?

scenes.fun_fact: The largest seed in the world is the coco de mer — it can weigh up to 25 kg!

scenes.title: Inside a Seed
scenes.narration: Let's look inside a seed! A seed is like a tiny suitcase that carries everything a baby plant needs to start its life. Click on each part to learn more.

dialogue.speaker: Tara
dialogue.text: Every seed has three important parts. Explore each one to understand how they work together!

hotspots.name: Seed Coat
hotspots.description: The seed coat is like a protective jacket. It is the hard outer covering that protects the seed from insects, diseases, and harsh weather until conditions are right for growing.
hotspots.fun_fact: Some seed coats are so tough that seeds can survive being eaten by animals and still grow!

hotspots.name: Cotyledon (Seed Leaves)
hotspots.description: Cotyledons are the food storage rooms of the seed. They contain starch and nutrients that feed the baby plant until it can make its own food using sunlight. Plants with one cotyledon are called monocots (like wheat), and those with two are called dicots (like beans).
hotspots.fun_fact: When you split a peanut in half, you can see the two cotyledons!

hotspots.name: Embryo (Baby Plant)
hotspots.description: The embryo is the baby plant hidden inside the seed. It has a tiny root called the radicle (which grows downward), a tiny shoot called the plumule (which grows upward), and the hypocotyl (the stem between them).
hotspots.fun_fact: The embryo stays dormant (sleeping) until it gets water, warmth, and air to wake up!

scenes.title: Germination Lab
scenes.narration: Germination is when a seed wakes up and begins to grow into a new plant. But seeds don't just grow anywhere — they need the right conditions. Let's experiment!

dialogue.speaker: Tara
dialogue.text: A seed needs three things to germinate: water, air, and warmth. Toggle each condition to see what happens!

experiment.title: What Does a Seed Need to Grow?
experiment.instructions: Toggle each condition ON or OFF and observe what happens to the seed.

conditions.name: Water
conditions.description: Water softens the seed coat and activates enzymes that start growth.

conditions.name: Air (Oxygen)
conditions.description: The seed needs oxygen to breathe and release energy for growth.

conditions.name: Warmth
conditions.description: Warmth speeds up the chemical reactions needed for the seed to grow.

conditions.name: Light
conditions.description: Most seeds do NOT need light to germinate — they sprout underground in the dark!

experiment.success_message: The seed has germinated! First the radicle (root) pushes down, then the plumule (shoot) pushes up through the soil!
experiment.failure_message: The seed cannot germinate without all the required conditions. Try again!

scenes.title: Stages of Germination
scenes.narration: Now let's watch how germination happens step by step. It's like a beautiful dance of nature!

dialogue.speaker: Buddi
dialogue.text: Watch me grow! First I drink water, then I crack open my coat, push my root down, and finally reach for the sky!

steps_display.title: Absorption
steps_display.description: The seed absorbs water and swells up. The seed coat becomes soft.

steps_display.title: Cracking
steps_display.description: The seed coat cracks open as the embryo inside grows bigger.

steps_display.title: Root Emerges
steps_display.description: The radicle (baby root) pushes out and grows downward into the soil.

steps_display.title: Shoot Emerges
steps_display.description: The plumule (baby shoot) grows upward, pushing through the soil toward light.

steps_display.title: Seedling
steps_display.description: The first leaves open and begin making food using sunlight. A new plant is born!

scenes.title: Seed Dispersal Adventure
scenes.narration: Seeds can't walk, but they travel far and wide! Nature has clever ways to spread seeds to new places. This is called seed dispersal. Can you match each seed to how it travels?

dialogue.speaker: Buddi
dialogue.text: I have friends who travel by wind, water, animals, and even explosions! Let's match them up!

activity.title: Match the Seed to Its Travel Method
activity.instructions: Select the correct dispersal method for each seed/plant.

pairs.hint: These seeds have fluffy parachutes that catch the breeze.
pairs.explanation: Dandelion seeds have light, feathery structures that let them float on the wind to faraway places.

pairs.hint: This seed has a hard waterproof shell and can float.
pairs.explanation: Coconuts have a waterproof outer shell and can float across oceans to reach new islands!

pairs.hint: These seeds have tiny hooks that stick to fur or clothes.
pairs.explanation: Burdock seeds have hook-like structures that cling to animal fur or human clothing, getting a free ride to new locations.

pairs.hint: The fruit bursts open suddenly when touched or ripe.
pairs.explanation: Balsam pods build up pressure as they ripen. When touched, they burst open and fling seeds in all directions!

pairs.hint: These seeds have wing-like structures that spin as they fall.
pairs.explanation: Maple seeds have papery wings that make them spin like tiny helicopters, carrying them away from the parent tree.

pairs.hint: Animals eat the tasty fruit and the seed passes through them.
pairs.explanation: Animals eat the sweet mango fruit and drop or excrete the seed far from the parent tree.

scenes.title: Vegetative Propagation Garden
scenes.narration: Did you know that some plants can grow new plants WITHOUT seeds? This is called vegetative propagation. Parts of the plant like stems, roots, and leaves can grow into entirely new plants!

dialogue.speaker: Tara
dialogue.text: Nature is full of surprises! Let's explore different ways plants can reproduce without seeds.

hotspots.name: Stem Tuber — Potato
hotspots.description: A potato is a stem tuber — an underground stem that stores food. The 'eyes' on a potato are buds. When you plant a piece of potato with an eye, a new plant grows from it!
hotspots.fun_fact: One potato plant can produce 5-10 new potatoes underground.

hotspots.name: Leaf Budding — Bryophyllum
hotspots.description: Bryophyllum (Mother of Thousands) has tiny baby plants growing along the edges of its leaves! These babies fall off and grow into new plants wherever they land.
hotspots.fun_fact: A single Bryophyllum leaf can produce dozens of baby plants along its edges!

hotspots.name: Rhizome — Ginger
hotspots.description: Ginger grows from a rhizome — a thick underground stem that grows horizontally. New shoots and roots sprout from the rhizome to form new plants.
hotspots.fun_fact: Turmeric, banana, and lotus also grow from rhizomes!

hotspots.name: Runner — Strawberry
hotspots.description: Strawberry plants send out long stems called runners that grow along the ground. Where a runner touches the soil, it puts down roots and grows a new plant!
hotspots.fun_fact: One strawberry plant can produce many runners, creating a whole family of connected plants.

hotspots.name: Stem Cutting — Rose
hotspots.description: You can grow a new rose plant by cutting a healthy stem and planting it in moist soil. The stem grows new roots and becomes an independent plant. This is called a cutting.
hotspots.fun_fact: Many garden plants like hibiscus, money plant, and croton are grown from stem cuttings.

scenes.title: Farmer Kisan's Field
scenes.narration: Farmer Kisan grows food for us all! Farming is not just planting seeds — it involves many careful steps from preparing the soil to storing the harvest. Let's follow the journey of a crop!

dialogue.speaker: Farmer Kisan
dialogue.text: Namaste! I'm Farmer Kisan. Growing crops is like taking care of a baby — you need patience and the right steps. Let me show you how we grow wheat!

activity.title: Put the Farming Steps in Order
activity.instructions: Drag and arrange these farming steps in the correct order from start to finish.

steps.name: Ploughing (Tilling)
steps.description: The soil is turned and loosened using a plough. This mixes nutrients, adds air, and makes it easy for roots to grow.

steps.name: Sowing
steps.description: Seeds are planted in the prepared soil at the right depth and spacing. Today, seed drills help farmers sow seeds evenly.

steps.name: Irrigation
steps.description: Crops need water at the right time. Farmers use canals, sprinklers, or drip irrigation to water their fields.

steps.name: Adding Fertilizers
steps.description: Plants need nutrients to grow well. Farmers add manure (natural) or fertilizers to enrich the soil.

steps.name: Weeding
steps.description: Unwanted plants (weeds) compete with crops for water and nutrients. They must be removed by hand or with tools.

steps.name: Harvesting
steps.description: When crops are fully grown and ripe, they are cut and collected. This is called harvesting.

steps.name: Storage
steps.description: Harvested grains are dried and stored in granaries or silos, protected from moisture and pests.

scenes.title: Chapter Challenge
scenes.narration: You've learned so much about plants! Before the final quiz, let's test your understanding with some quick-fire questions.

dialogue.speaker: Tara
dialogue.text: Time to put your knowledge to the test! Answer these true-or-false questions. Think carefully!

questions.statement: A seed needs light to germinate.
questions.explanation: Most seeds germinate in the dark, underground. They need water, air, and warmth — not light!

questions.statement: The cotyledon stores food for the baby plant.
questions.explanation: Correct! Cotyledons are the food storage parts that nourish the embryo until it can make its own food.

questions.statement: Coconut seeds are dispersed by wind.
questions.explanation: Coconuts are dispersed by water! They float across rivers and oceans to reach new places.

questions.statement: Potato is an example of vegetative propagation.
questions.explanation: Yes! Potatoes grow from stem tubers — new plants sprout from the 'eyes' without any seeds.

questions.statement: The radicle grows upward to become the shoot.
questions.explanation: The radicle grows DOWNWARD to become the root. The plumule grows upward to become the shoot.

questions.statement: Weeding means removing unwanted plants from the field.
questions.explanation: Correct! Weeds compete with crops for water, nutrients, and sunlight, so farmers remove them.

scenes.title: Final Quiz — Seed Explorer
scenes.narration: This is your final quiz for the chapter! Answer all questions to earn your Seed Explorer badge.

dialogue.speaker: Buddi
dialogue.text: You've come so far! Let's see if you can become a certified Seed Explorer. Good luck!

questions.question: What is the protective outer covering of a seed called?
questions.explanation: The seed coat is the hard outer layer that protects the seed from damage, insects, and weather.

questions.question: Which part of the seed stores food for the baby plant?
questions.explanation: The cotyledon stores nutrients like starch and proteins that feed the embryo during germination.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT needed for seed germination?
questions.explanation: Most seeds germinate underground without light. They need water, air (oxygen), and warmth.

questions.question: How are dandelion seeds dispersed?
questions.explanation: Dandelion seeds have light, feathery parachutes that catch the wind and float to new places.

questions.question: Which plant reproduces through leaf budding?
questions.explanation: Bryophyllum (Mother of Thousands) produces tiny baby plants along the edges of its leaves.

questions.question: What is the correct first step in farming?
questions.explanation: Ploughing (tilling) comes first — the soil must be loosened and prepared before seeds are sown.

questions.question: The part of the embryo that grows into the root is called:
questions.explanation: The radicle is the embryonic root — it grows downward into the soil to anchor the plant and absorb water.

questions.question: Which is an example of a stem tuber?
questions.explanation: Potato is a stem tuber — it grows underground and stores food. The 'eyes' are buds for new plants.

questions.question: Balsam (Touch-me-not) disperses its seeds by:
questions.explanation: Balsam pods burst open when touched or ripe, flinging seeds away from the parent plant!

questions.question: Which of these is a natural method of adding nutrients to soil?
questions.explanation: Manure is a natural way to add nutrients to soil. It comes from decomposed plant or animal waste.

scenes.title: Chapter Summary
scenes.narration: Congratulations, explorer! You have completed the chapter on Plants. Let's review what you learned on this amazing adventure.

dialogue.speaker: Tara
dialogue.text: What a journey! You now know the secrets of seeds, how plants grow, travel, and how farmers feed the world. I'm so proud of you!

summary_points.title: Seed Structure
summary_points.text: A seed has three parts: seed coat (protection), cotyledon (food storage), and embryo (baby plant).

summary_points.title: Germination
summary_points.text: Seeds need water, air, and warmth to germinate. The radicle grows down, the plumule grows up.

summary_points.title: Seed Dispersal
summary_points.text: Seeds travel by wind (dandelion), water (coconut), animals (burdock), and explosion (balsam).

summary_points.title: Vegetative Propagation
summary_points.text: Some plants grow without seeds — from tubers (potato), leaves (bryophyllum), rhizomes (ginger), runners (strawberry), and cuttings (rose).

summary_points.title: Agriculture
summary_points.text: Farming involves: ploughing, sowing, irrigation, fertilizing, weeding, harvesting, and storage.

scenes.title: Badge Awarded — Seed Explorer!
scenes.narration: You did it! You have earned the Seed Explorer badge! You now know all about seeds, germination, dispersal, vegetative propagation, and farming.

dialogue.speaker: Buddi
dialogue.text: We did it together! You helped me understand everything about growing. Thank you, Seed Explorer! See you in the next adventure!

badge.name: Seed Explorer
badge.description: Awarded for completing Chapter 1: More About Plants
badge.criteria: Complete all scenes and pass the quiz
